claimed_contribution: The paper introduces and validates distinct cue-based constructs and dictionaries for frontline solving work, relational work, and displayed affect, using verbal and nonverbal data from service interactions.
It provides a dynamic, within-interaction analysis of problem-solving effectiveness, estimating how the effect of frontline solving work on CSAT evolves over time.

It shows that excessive relational talk and positive affect displays can reduce the effectiveness of solving work, offering a counterpoint to the common "more is better" view of relational behavior in recovery contexts.
context: The study examines frontline service-failure and complaint interactions in commercial air travel, where employees and customers negotiate solutions under time pressure at check-in counters, gates, and in-flight settings. Empirical contexts include easyJet operations in UK airports and Southwest operations in US airports, plus a controlled airline-passenger experiment.
design_and_data: Study 1 uses fly-on-the-wall video data from the TV series Airline, capturing naturalistic frontline problem-solving episodes in UK and US airports. From 111 usable interactions, 9 were reserved for dictionary development and 102 were analyzed, segmented into 373 time-ordered segments and coded thin slices for nonverbal cues; the authors built and validated dictionaries for solving and relational verbal cues and coded displayed affect and CSAT from nonverbal behavior. They estimated dynamic effects with a random-parameters panel model including interactions with time and robustness checks. Study 2 is a 2 x 2 x 2 between-subjects experiment (high/low solving work x high/low relational work x missed flight/lost baggage) with 568 recent airline travelers from a US online panel using controlled audio scenarios.
main_findings: Frontline solving work has a positive effect on CSAT, and this effect increases as the interaction unfolds.
Higher levels of frontline relational work weaken the positive solving work-CSAT relationship over time, and at high relational work the solving effect can become nonsignificant.
Higher levels of positive displayed affect similarly weaken the solving work-CSAT relationship over time, and solving work still improves CSAT when problem-resolution outcomes are held constant.
one_line_summary: Using naturalistic airline interaction videos and a controlled experiment, the paper finds that frontline solving work increasingly raises CSAT over time, but excessive relational talk or positive affect displays reduce that benefit.
relation_to_prior_work: extends complaint-handling and service-recovery research by shifting from static pre/post evaluations to dynamic within-interaction process analysis using both verbal and nonverbal cues; it also challenges the assumption that more relational behavior and positive affect are always beneficial during service failure resolution.
research_gap: Prior service recovery and complaint-handling work mostly studies customer states before complaints or outcomes after recovery, with limited attention to real-time problem-solving processes during interactions. Existing studies also provide little evidence on time-varying effects and on how nonverbal affect cues jointly shape the effectiveness of frontline solving work.
research_question: How does frontline solving work influence customer satisfaction during ongoing problem-solving interactions after service failures? Does this influence change over time as interactions progress, and is it moderated by frontline relational verbal work and displayed nonverbal affect?
